{{Short description="Model that describes gases as a large number of particles in random motion"}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Kinetic theory of gases
| image = Translational motion.gif
| caption = Particles of a gas in random motion. The kinetic theory relates macroscopic properties (pressure, temperature) to microscopic motion.
| topics = [[Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution]], [[Equipartition theorem]], [[Mean free path]], [[Brownian motion]]
}}

The '''kinetic theory of gases''' is a simple, historically significant [[classical mechanics|classical]] model of the [[ideal gas|thermodynamic behavior of gases]], with which many principal concepts of [[thermodynamics]] were established. The model describes a [[gas]] as a large number of identical [[sub-microscopic|submicroscopic]] particles ([[atom]]s or [[molecule]]s), all of which are in constant, rapid, random [[motion (physics)|motion]].

The theory was developed by [[James Clerk Maxwell|Maxwell]], [[Ludwig Boltzmann|Boltzmann]], and [[Rudolf Clausius|Clausius]] in the 19th century.

== Assumptions ==
The basic model assumes:
# The gas consists of a large number of identical molecules
# Molecules are in constant, random motion
# The volume of the molecules is negligible compared to the container
# Collisions between molecules and with walls are perfectly [[elastic collision|elastic]]
# No intermolecular forces except during collisions
# Newton's laws apply to molecular motion

== Pressure from molecular collisions ==
The [[pressure]] exerted by a gas arises from molecular collisions with the container walls. For a cubic container of side <math>L</math>:
:<math>P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{Nm\langle v^2 \rangle}{V}</math>

where <math>N</math> is the number of molecules, <math>m</math> is the molecular mass, and <math>\langle v^2 \rangle</math> is the mean square speed.

== Kinetic interpretation of temperature ==
Comparing with the [[ideal gas law]] <math>PV = Nk_B T</math>:
:<math>\frac{1}{2}m\langle v^2 \rangle = \frac{3}{2}k_B T</math>

The average translational kinetic energy per molecule is:
:<math>\langle K_{\text{trans}} \rangle = \frac{3}{2}k_B T</math>

[[Temperature]] is thus a direct measure of the average kinetic energy of molecules.

== Root-mean-square speed ==
:<math>v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\langle v^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{\frac{3k_B T}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}}</math>

For nitrogen at room temperature: <math>v_{\text{rms}} \approx 515\,\text{m/s}</math>.

== Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution ==
{{Main|Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution}}
The probability distribution of molecular speeds in thermal equilibrium:
:<math>f(v) = 4\pi \left(\frac{m}{2\pi k_B T}\right)^{3/2} v^2 e^{-mv^2/(2k_B T)}</math>

Key speeds:
* Most probable: <math>v_p = \sqrt{2k_B T/m}</math>
* Mean: <math>\langle v \rangle = \sqrt{8k_B T/(\pi m)}</math>
* RMS: <math>v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{3k_B T/m}</math>

Ratio: <math>v_p : \langle v \rangle : v_{\text{rms}} = 1 : 1.128 : 1.225</math>

== Mean free path ==
{{Main|Mean free path}}
The average distance traveled between collisions:
:<math>\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 n}</math>
where <math>d</math> is the molecular diameter and <math>n = N/V</math> is the number density.

For air at STP: <math>\lambda \approx 68\,\text{nm}</math>.

== Equipartition theorem ==
{{Main|Equipartition theorem}}
Each quadratic degree of freedom contributes <math>\frac{1}{2}k_B T</math> to the average energy. For a diatomic gas:
:<math>\langle E \rangle = \frac{5}{2}k_B T</math> (3 translational + 2 rotational)

== Transport phenomena ==
Kinetic theory explains:
* '''[[Viscosity]]:''' <math>\eta = \frac{1}{3}\rho\langle v \rangle \lambda</math>
* '''[[Thermal conductivity]]:''' <math>\kappa = \frac{1}{3}\rho c_v \langle v \rangle \lambda</math>
* '''[[Diffusion]]:''' <math>D = \frac{1}{3}\langle v \rangle \lambda</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution]]
* [[Equipartition theorem]]
* [[Brownian motion]]
* [[Statistical mechanics]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Kinetic theory of gases| ]]
[[Category:Thermodynamics]]
[[Category:Statistical mechanics]]
